content.text: LSP deep integration goes beyond the basic textDocument/definition and textDocument/references requests covered in introductory LSP use. It encompasses the advanced capabilities of the Language Server Protocol that give a coding agent semantic-depth understanding: call hierarchies, type hierarchies, semantic token classification, code actions with workspace-wide edits, inlay hints, and server-managed refactoring. These features transform the agent from a code reader into a code reasoner that understands the structural relationships between symbols across an entire codebase.

Deep integration also covers the operational concerns of running language servers in production agent sessions: multi-workspace configurations for polyglot monorepos, server lifecycle management including crash recovery, custom LSP extensions exposed by specific language servers (rust-analyzer, pyright), and workspace configuration APIs that let the agent tune server behaviour at runtime.

Basic LSP integration tells the agent where a symbol is defined and where it is used. Deep integration tells the agent how the codebase is structured: which functions call which other functions (call hierarchy), how classes relate through inheritance (type hierarchy), what type every token has (semantic tokens). This structural knowledge is the difference between an agent that edits code mechanically and one that understands the impact of its changes.

Code actions and workspace edits enable server-driven refactoring: instead of the agent manually computing all the text changes required by a rename or an extract-function operation, it asks the language server to compute the edits and then applies them atomically. This is both safer and more accurate than agent-generated text manipulation, because the server applies the same refactoring logic used by production IDEs.
callHierarchy/incomingCalls recursively answers which functions call a target, enabling the agent to trace execution paths and understand blast radius before making a change.
typeHierarchy/supertypes and subtypes answer where a class fits in the inheritance tree, revealing all concrete implementations of an interface.

textDocument/codeAction returns a list of refactoring operations the server can apply. The agent selects one and calls workspace/applyEdit, letting the server compute all required multi-file text changes.

textDocument/semanticTokens returns a type classification (class, function, variable, parameter, namespace, etc.) for every token in a file. This gives the agent a machine-readable semantic map of the code.

textDocument/inlayHint returns the type annotations that the language server would display inline — particularly useful for auto-annotating Python functions where return types and parameter types are inferred but not written.

rust-analyzer and pyright expose custom JSON-RPC methods beyond the standard LSP spec — macro expansion, package graph queries, type stubs — giving the agent language-specific power unavailable through standard requests.

Advanced LSP capabilities require correspondingly advanced server support. Not all language servers implement the full spec: callHierarchy is supported by pyright and rust-analyzer but not by all servers; typeHierarchy is available in TypeScript and Java servers but patchy elsewhere; semanticTokens is widely supported but the token type vocabularies differ between servers. The agent must check the server's advertised capabilities in the initialize response before calling advanced methods.

Code actions and workspace edits require the agent to implement the workspace/applyEdit handler — the server sends an applyEdit request to the client to apply changes, so the client must process this incoming request rather than just passively receiving it. This bidirectional flow (client calls server, server calls back to client) is more complex than the typical request-response pattern. In a coding agent, the handler must also update the agent's internal model of open documents to reflect the applied edits.

Call hierarchy resolution is a two-step process. First, the agent calls textDocument/prepareCallHierarchy at a symbol position. This returns a list of CallHierarchyItem objects — each represents a callable entity and contains its name, file URI, and range. Second, the agent calls callHierarchy/incomingCalls or callHierarchy/outgoingCalls with one of these items as input. The response is a list of CallHierarchyIncomingCall objects, each containing a CallHierarchyItem for the caller and the specific ranges within that caller where the call occurs. This can be called recursively to traverse the full call graph.

Semantic tokens use a compact binary encoding rather than per-token JSON objects. The server returns an array of integers where each group of five integers encodes one token: delta-line, delta-character, length, token type (index into the server's declared token type legend), and token modifiers (bitmask). The agent decodes this array by maintaining a running (line, character) position and applying deltas, then maps the integer type index to the string type name using the legend from the server's capabilities.

Code actions work as follows: the agent calls textDocument/codeAction with a range and a context (which diagnostics apply in that range). The server returns a list of CodeAction or Command objects. For CodeAction objects with a kind of refactor.*, the agent can apply the action by either executing its embedded WorkspaceEdit directly (via workspace/applyEdit) or by calling workspace/executeCommand if the action specifies a command. The WorkspaceEdit contains a map from file URI to a list of TextEdit objects, which the agent applies in document-order from bottom to top to preserve offsets.

Multi-workspace setups use the workspace/workspaceFolders capability. After initialization, the agent can add or remove workspace folders via a workspace/didChangeWorkspaceFolders notification. Each folder is treated as a separate root by the language server, allowing a single server instance to cover multiple sub-projects in a monorepo. Some servers (pyright, typescript-language-server) can also discover individual project configurations (pyrightconfig.json, tsconfig.json) within each folder and handle them independently.

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram showing the coding agent using advanced LSP capabilities: call hierarchy, type hierarchy, semantic tokens, code actions, plus operational concerns like inlay hints, server lifecycle, multi-workspace, and custom extensions.

content.text: The top row shows the primary advanced navigation and analysis capabilities. The bottom row shows the operational and lifecycle capabilities. Together, they form the full surface area of a deep LSP integration. The agent accesses all of these through the same JSON-RPC client used for basic LSP, but each capability group has its own request/notification flow.

Use call hierarchy when the agent must understand the impact of changing a function's signature or behaviour: it needs to find not just direct callers but indirect callers at any depth. Use type hierarchy when working with polymorphic code where the agent needs to know which concrete classes implement an interface or which base classes a class inherits from.

Use code actions for any refactoring that the language server supports natively — rename, extract function, inline variable, add missing import, implement interface methods. Letting the server compute the edit is more reliable than the agent generating the edit text manually. Use semantic tokens when building a structural map of a file for purposes like code summarisation or when the agent needs to distinguish between identically named symbols of different kinds (a class named Buffer versus a variable named buffer).
Check capabilities in the initialize response. Read serverCapabilities.callHierarchyProvider, typeHierarchyProvider, semanticTokensProvider, and codeActionProvider before issuing those requests.
For code actions, implement a workspace/applyEdit request handler on the client side. The server will call this to apply refactoring edits.
For semantic tokens, extract the token type legend from the server's capabilities and build a decoder.
For multi-workspace, send workspace/didChangeWorkspaceFolders notifications when the agent discovers new project roots.
For crash recovery, implement a supervisor that monitors the server process exit code and restarts with a new session on non-zero exit.

# Call hierarchy: find all functions that (transitively) call a target
import asyncio
from typing import Any


async def get_incoming_call_hierarchy(
    lsp: "LspClient",  # the async client from the LSP integration topic
    file_path: str,
    line: int,
    character: int,
    max_depth: int = 3,
) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Return all callers of the symbol at the given position, up to max_depth levels.
    Returns a flat list of {caller_name, caller_file, caller_line, depth} dicts.
    """
    from pathlib import Path

    # Step 1: prepare the call hierarchy item
    items = await lsp._request("textDocument/prepareCallHierarchy", {
        "textDocument": {"uri": Path(file_path).as_uri()},
        "position": {"line": line, "character": character},
    })
    if not items:
        return []

    results: list[dict] = []

    async def recurse(item: dict, depth: int) -> None:
        if depth > max_depth:
            return
        incoming = await lsp._request("callHierarchy/incomingCalls", {"item": item})
        if not incoming:
            return
        for call in incoming:
            caller = call["from"]
            caller_uri = caller.get("uri", "")
            caller_line = caller.get("selectionRange", {}).get("start", {}).get("line", 0)
            results.append({
                "caller_name": caller.get("name", ""),
                "caller_file": caller_uri.removeprefix("file://"),
                "caller_line": caller_line,
                "depth": depth,
            })
            await recurse(caller, depth + 1)

    await recurse(items[0], depth=1)
    return results


async def get_type_subtypes(
    lsp: "LspClient",
    file_path: str,
    line: int,
    character: int,
) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """Find all classes that inherit from the class at the given position."""
    from pathlib import Path

    items = await lsp._request("textDocument/prepareTypeHierarchy", {
        "textDocument": {"uri": Path(file_path).as_uri()},
        "position": {"line": line, "character": character},
    })
    if not items:
        return []

    subtypes = await lsp._request("typeHierarchy/subtypes", {"item": items[0]})
    if not subtypes:
        return []

    return [
        {
            "name": sub.get("name", ""),
            "file": sub.get("uri", "").removeprefix("file://"),
            "line": sub.get("selectionRange", {}).get("start", {}).get("line", 0),
        }
        for sub in subtypes
    ]

# Semantic tokens decoder + code action applier
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class SemanticToken:
    line: int
    character: int
    length: int
    token_type: str
    modifiers: list[str]


def decode_semantic_tokens(
    data: list[int],
    token_types: list[str],
    token_modifiers: list[str],
) -> list[SemanticToken]:
    """Decode LSP's compact integer-encoded semantic token array."""
    tokens: list[SemanticToken] = []
    current_line = 0
    current_char = 0

    for i in range(0, len(data), 5):
        delta_line = data[i]
        delta_char = data[i + 1]
        length = data[i + 2]
        type_idx = data[i + 3]
        mod_bitmask = data[i + 4]

        if delta_line > 0:
            current_line += delta_line
            current_char = delta_char
        else:
            current_char += delta_char

        t_type = token_types[type_idx] if type_idx < len(token_types) else "unknown"
        mods = [
            token_modifiers[j]
            for j in range(len(token_modifiers))
            if mod_bitmask & (1 << j)
        ]
        tokens.append(SemanticToken(current_line, current_char, length, t_type, mods))

    return tokens


async def get_semantic_tokens_for_file(
    lsp: "LspClient",
    file_path: str,
    capabilities: dict,
) -> list[SemanticToken]:
    """Fetch and decode semantic tokens for a file."""
    legend = capabilities.get("semanticTokensProvider", {}).get("legend", {})
    token_types: list[str] = legend.get("tokenTypes", [])
    token_modifiers: list[str] = legend.get("tokenModifiers", [])

    result = await lsp._request("textDocument/semanticTokens/full", {
        "textDocument": {"uri": Path(file_path).as_uri()},
    })
    if not result or "data" not in result:
        return []

    return decode_semantic_tokens(result["data"], token_types, token_modifiers)


async def apply_code_action(
    lsp: "LspClient",
    file_path: str,
    start_line: int,
    end_line: int,
    action_kind_prefix: str = "refactor",
) -> str:
    """
    Request code actions for a range and apply the first matching one.
    Returns a description of what was applied.
    """
    uri = Path(file_path).as_uri()
    file_diags = lsp.diagnostics.get(uri, [])

    actions = await lsp._request("textDocument/codeAction", {
        "textDocument": {"uri": uri},
        "range": {
            "start": {"line": start_line, "character": 0},
            "end": {"line": end_line, "character": 0},
        },
        "context": {"diagnostics": file_diags},
    })

    if not actions:
        return "No code actions available for this range."

    # Find first action matching the requested kind
    target = next(
        (a for a in actions if isinstance(a, dict) and
         a.get("kind", "").startswith(action_kind_prefix)),
        None,
    )
    if target is None:
        available = [a.get("kind", "(no kind)") for a in actions if isinstance(a, dict)]
        return f"No {action_kind_prefix} action found. Available: {available}"

    # Apply the embedded WorkspaceEdit if present
    if "edit" in target:
        edit = target["edit"]
        for file_uri, text_edits in (edit.get("changes") or {}).items():
            path = file_uri.removeprefix("file://")
            source = Path(path).read_text()
            lines = source.splitlines(keepends=True)
            for te in sorted(text_edits, key=lambda e: e["range"]["start"]["line"], reverse=True):
                s = te["range"]["start"]
                e = te["range"]["end"]
                new_text = te["newText"]
                # Simplified: full-line replacement
                lines[s["line"]:e["line"] + 1] = [new_text]
            Path(path).write_text("".join(lines))

    title = target.get("title", "(unnamed action)")
    return f"Applied code action: {title}"
The following example demonstrates how a coding agent uses semantic tokens to build a type-annotated map of a Python file, distinguishing classes from functions from parameters.
The next example shows server lifecycle management with automatic crash recovery and restart.

# Language server supervisor with crash recovery
import asyncio
import logging
from pathlib import Path

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class LspSupervisor:
    """Wraps LspClient with automatic restart on server crash."""

    def __init__(self, command: list[str], root_path: str, max_restarts: int = 3):
        self.command = command
        self.root_path = root_path
        self.max_restarts = max_restarts
        self._client: "LspClient | None" = None
        self._restart_count = 0
        self._open_files: dict[str, str] = {}  # uri -> content

    async def start(self) -> None:
        self._client = LspClient()
        await self._client.start(self.command, self.root_path)
        # Re-open any files that were open before a restart
        for path_str, content in self._open_files.items():
            await self._client._notify("textDocument/didOpen", {
                "textDocument": {
                    "uri": Path(path_str).as_uri(),
                    "languageId": "python",
                    "version": 1,
                    "text": content,
                }
            })

    async def ensure_alive(self) -> "LspClient":
        """Return the client, restarting the server if it has crashed."""
        if self._client is None or self._client._proc.returncode is not None:
            if self._restart_count >= self.max_restarts:
                raise RuntimeError("Language server crashed too many times")
            self._restart_count += 1
            logger.warning("Language server crashed. Restarting (attempt %d)...", self._restart_count)
            await asyncio.sleep(0.5 * self._restart_count)  # backoff
            await self.start()
        return self._client

    async def did_open(self, file_path: str) -> None:
        content = Path(file_path).read_text()
        self._open_files[file_path] = content
        client = await self.ensure_alive()
        await client.did_open(file_path)

    async def get_definition(self, path: str, line: int, char: int) -> list[dict]:
        client = await self.ensure_alive()
        return await client.get_definition(path, line, char)

A coding agent is tasked with extracting a large Python class method into a standalone function. It first uses the call hierarchy to understand which other methods call the target, confirming it is only called from one place. It then calls textDocument/codeAction on the method's range to obtain the extract-function code action from pyright. It applies the workspace edit returned by pyright, which handles creating the new function, updating the call site, and moving any captured variables to parameters. Finally, it calls get_diagnostics to confirm that pyright reports no type errors in the modified files.

# Extract-function refactoring via LSP code action
async def extract_method_to_function(
    lsp: "LspClient",
    file_path: str,
    method_start_line: int,
    method_end_line: int,
) -> str:
    """
    Use LSP code actions to extract a method body to a standalone function.
    This delegates the complex text transformation to the language server.
    """
    # Step 1: check call hierarchy to understand impact
    callers = await get_incoming_call_hierarchy(
        lsp, file_path, method_start_line, character=4, max_depth=2
    )
    caller_summary = (
        f"{len(callers)} callers found" if callers
        else "no callers found"
    )

    # Step 2: request code actions for the method range
    result = await apply_code_action(
        lsp,
        file_path,
        start_line=method_start_line,
        end_line=method_end_line,
        action_kind_prefix="refactor.extract",
    )

    # Step 3: verify no new errors
    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)  # allow diagnostics to arrive
    uri = f"file://{file_path}"
    errors = [
        d for d in lsp.diagnostics.get(uri, []) if d.get("severity") == 1
    ]
    error_summary = "no errors" if not errors else f"{len(errors)} errors"

    return (
        f"Extract-function refactoring complete.\n"
        f"  Call hierarchy: {caller_summary}\n"
        f"  Action result: {result}\n"
        f"  Post-edit diagnostics: {error_summary}"
    )

A common mistake with code actions is not implementing the workspace/applyEdit server-to-client request handler. When the agent calls workspace/executeCommand for an action, the server sends workspace/applyEdit back to the client as a request expecting a response. If the client ignores this incoming request, the refactoring silently fails because the edit is never applied. The JSON-RPC reader loop must distinguish between responses to outgoing requests and incoming requests from the server.

For semantic tokens, a common error is treating the integer data as per-token absolute positions rather than delta-encoded positions. Each token's line and character are deltas relative to the previous token — not absolute positions. Decoding without applying the deltas correctly produces tokens at wildly wrong positions. The reference implementation must maintain mutable (line, char) state as it iterates through the token array.
Always verify that the server advertised the capability in its initialize response. Calling an unsupported method returns a JSON-RPC MethodNotFound error that will stall the request future.

The JSON-RPC reader must handle incoming requests (messages with an id but where the client did not originate them), not just responses and notifications. workspace/applyEdit and workspace/executeCommand are the most common. Respond with a result object even if the action was a no-op.
Recursive callHierarchy/incomingCalls can traverse enormous graphs in large codebases. Always enforce a max_depth limit and a max_nodes limit to prevent the traversal from running indefinitely.

When a code action provides a WorkspaceEdit, apply it directly rather than regenerating the edit text. The server's edit is computed by the same analysis engine that understands the full type graph; the agent's text manipulation is error-prone for complex multi-file changes.
Do not add all workspace folders at once if the project is large. Add folders on demand as the agent navigates into them to avoid triggering a full re-index on every session start.

Call hierarchy traversal has O(n) query cost where n is the number of callers at each depth level. On a codebase where a utility function has 500 direct callers and max_depth is set to 3, the traversal can issue thousands of requests. Cache call hierarchy items within a session to avoid re-querying the same item when it appears as a caller in multiple branches.

Semantic tokens for large files can return large integer arrays (a 1000-line file with dense code may produce 5000-10000 tokens, each encoded as 5 integers = 50000 integer array). The decode operation is O(n) and fast in practice, but the payload size increases RPC latency. Use textDocument/semanticTokens/range instead of textDocument/semanticTokens/full when only a portion of the file is needed.
Cache call hierarchy items, type hierarchy items, and workspace/symbol results within a session. These are stable as long as the codebase has not changed. Invalidate on didChange notifications.
Use textDocument/semanticTokens/range and textDocument/inlayHint with an explicit range to avoid fetching tokens or hints for the entire file when only a specific section is needed.

Code actions produce WorkspaceEdit objects that may modify many files simultaneously. Before applying a multi-file workspace edit, the agent should validate that all target file URIs are within the expected project root. A malicious or buggy language server could theoretically produce edits targeting system files outside the project. Additionally, validate that no edit deletes an entire file (an empty text replacing the full file content) without explicit user confirmation.

Language servers that support custom extensions (rust-analyzer's $ra_ methods, pyright's pyright/ methods) are accessing internal compiler state. These extensions are not part of the stable LSP spec and may change between server versions. Pin the language server version in the agent's dependency manifest and test against the specific version; an upgrade can silently break custom method calls. Monitor the server's stderr stream for deprecation warnings about custom methods.

Deep LSP integration gives a coding agent call hierarchy traversal, type hierarchy navigation, semantic token classification, and server-computed refactoring edits — the structural understanding layer above basic definition and reference lookups.
Always check serverCapabilities in the initialize response before calling advanced methods; capability support varies significantly between language servers.
Semantic tokens use delta-encoded integer arrays; maintain a running (line, char) cursor when decoding, not absolute positions.
Code actions require handling workspace/applyEdit as an incoming server-to-client request; a client that only processes responses will silently ignore refactoring edits.
Prefer server-computed WorkspaceEdits for refactoring over agent-generated text manipulation; the server's type-aware edit is more accurate for complex multi-file changes.
Bound call hierarchy traversal with max_depth and max_nodes limits; on large codebases, utility functions can have thousands of indirect callers.
Implement a supervisor with crash recovery for production use; language servers can crash on malformed code or unsupported project structures.
Custom LSP extensions (rust-analyzer, pyright) are not part of the stable spec; pin server versions and test against them to avoid silent breakage on upgrade.
